quotes.quote: Over the years, like a lot of others, I've built a collection of training aids. Some were helpful in improving my game, some were just OK, and some were a stupid waste of money. Looking back, I haven't recommended many to my golfing friends. The one I have recommended is the Sklz Gold Flex. I bought it to use as a warm-up device before a round, and it works well at that. Eventually, I realized that it's a good swing trainer for me.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Multiple training aids, Sklz Gold Flex

quotes.quote: My opinion is it's a waste of money. Most instructors will tell you to just get a rubber ball.

I've been getting a lot of targeted ads for this training aid, and it aligns with what my lessons I had taken want me to try and work on. I have used the Smart Ball in the past, but I like the way this forms to the arms a little better, and I like the alignment stick additions as well.

I have the medicus 2000 dual hinge, and for me it was worth the price of admision. I still swing it almost everyday to keep my swing in sync. It helped me with my tempo, and getting the club in the correct position through out my back swing

I have a momentus. I like it for warming up and helping me stretch out. I knew Jim Sorenson (Momentus Inventor) when he was a teaching pro at Golf World... I think the momentus is good for strength and stretching, but I don't think you will suddenly be hitting it like they claim.

gimmicks ...a teacher will help more ..and practice!!!!! if you think swinging one of those for 10-20 mins is going to help you your wrong...although the only swing thing i would suggest is a swing weight or those heavy yellow clubs to help you loosen up

I have tried those Swing Link things (David Leadbetter and Peter Jacobsen) where they fasten your arms to your body. I think it can give people that don't know how to make a shoulder turn a good idea of how to use your body to swing the club instead of just your hands and arms. But for anyone other than a beginner they don't make much sense as they put your arms in a very weird position.
But the best thing out there is a good Pro with good camera equipment and a few hundred balls a day.
Well I bought a swing trainer today. It is called Inside Approach Slice correction trainer By Golf Digest. I got out to use it a little today. It seems to help, even with practice balls.

I have built many things as well as purchased many. The only one I would actually recommend would be the Orange Whip. Similar to the Sklz Gold Flex. These are something you can always use no matter what your ability is. Meaning you won't grow out of it.
A teacher - Best swing aid there is - After that certainly something for alignment and a somewhat trained eye to be sure you are doing it right - feet, hips, shoulders down the intended line.
Ive recommended Superspeed to lots of people, no one has taken me up on it. Despite the fact that they often comment on how much farther I'm hitting it.
Ive recommended personal launch monitors - if you know the windows you're trying to hit or have a place to practice with gamer like golf balls they are very helpful.

i think there are a number of training aids that are good if people know what they are trying to accomplish, pick the right one and then actually use them as prescribed. From my experience that combination is highly unlikely.
The best training aid above all is the Orange Whip. I use it daily

Best one I have used is a shoe box... placed outside the ball line maybe 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch from the toe of your club at address. It perfectly lines up with your target line and you immediately know if you are swinging inside-to-square-to-inside.
quotes.solution_mentioned: DIY shoe box swing path trainer

quotes.quote: Other than that, I can recommend a launch monitor. I purchased a SkyTrak. But, even the less expensive ones are useful. I have the SwingCaddie SC100. You can hit into a net or hit in your yard or another open space -- especially drilling on partial swing wedges and scoring irons in the 20 - 100 yard range.
Swingyde: probably the best training aid I own. When my ball flight gets erratic, it usually fixes the problem.

I've answered this question many times on here and my answer never waivers. Big Moss putting green and a putting mirror. You can use them all year, regardless of weather and you can do multiple drills that impact the club you use most often during a round.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Big Moss putting green, putting mirror

quotes.quote: This is really the most important aspect to any training aid, commit to using it! if you aren't going to incorporate into your practice routine on a regular basis you are wasting your money. I spend at least 15-minutes 3+ times a week on my practice green.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Consistent practice routine with training aids

quotes.quote: Orange Whip fan here, most recent aid is a putting mirror. So simple to use and it gets your eye line and shoulders square to target line. Love it.

Same two for me. Before teeing off my buddies all take turns with my Orange Whip to warm up. I use my daily and hit it past them regularly. Tried to convince them to get their own, but don't mind being the big stick. Low putter in the group too and only one with a mirror. Go figure.
I've had good success with the Tour Striker Planemate and Smartball and a golf club for stretching to increase flexibility and ability to turn.
I like the Lag Shot, really teaches lag
Jury is still out on this one for me - but I am sticking with it and the videos have been worth the purchase price, for me at least.
I haven't come across anything that I would recommend to a friend until I tried Super Speed Training... I highly recommend it. It works. You can find many reviews in this forum.
For the ball yes it is a waste of money. The real value comes in the instruction that comes with it. I know the Tour Striker version has a ton of videos for different drill uses.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Tour Striker ball with video instruction

quotes.quote: I am not a Tour Striker shill but I have been to the Academy and have worked with Martin Chuck and his instructors.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Tour Striker Academy, Martin Chuck instruction

quotes.quote: Due to a recent wrist injury I got a sklz smart glove. A bunch of my high handicap friends got it and love it.
CONDITIONAL: Tour Striker Plane mate - if you are over the top, all arms, or struggle with flipping the club or thins and shanks.

You should try cross-fitting. I have been playing golf for five years now, and I can easily say that I have always improved my skill, but the only thing I was lacking was the physique. I decided to change that, and now I've been consistently training for seven months, and I've never felt so good. My game improved drastically.

I'm a 72 year old with a 16 handicap. I have used the Blue Brick for just two 1 hr practice sessions and have turned my drives from 200 yd weak fades to 240 yd slight draws EVERY single time!!! This device has helped me 'feel' what it means to be on plane and properly shallow the club; something I have never been able to get a handle on until now.

I shot a personal best 70 this summer and lowered my handicap down to a 1 using the Top Speed Golf system along with the Blue Brick. Can't wait to make even more progress this fall! TSG combined with the Blue Brick is the best lesson/training combo I've ever used.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Top Speed Golf system, Blue Brick

quotes.quote: Love mine. Seeing great results. The wedge impact position is MONEY. Wedge game improved dramatically.
If I had to give this brick a title, it would be 'I learned how to hit draws in one range session.' This brick is incredible and I would highly recommend this to any golfer of any level.
I normally think golf aids are a waste of money or don't work. The Blue Brick is super high quality. It has a decently thick metal base plate too.
my dad has the momentus with 2 joints. it seems to make sense with how you are suppose to use it and how its suppose to help... but i think some lessons would be a better way to go

The only one I would give the nod to, is the momentus power hittier. The weighted clubs help you ingrain a good swing. If your timing is off, you'll be off balance, but if it's right, you have a nice smooth finish. Really helps you get warmed up on the course. Plus, If you swing that thing long enough, your swing speed will increase a little.
swing weight helped me shorten my swing to parallel cuz if i went to far my swing became unbalanced etc so it made me shorten my swing to parallel to keep a balanced swing

I also use the Sklz Gold Flex as a warm up, golf swing trainer, and for exercise. In addition I would recommend a Strike Right Golf Swing Trainer. If you want to train an under, out and up swing path, the Strike Right will help do that as it also helps fix the over the top move.
quotes.solution_mentioned: Sklz Gold Flex, Strike Right Golf Swing Trainer

quotes.quote: I am a big fan of putting gates for the putter and ball to make sure I am hitting the center of the face and starting the ball online. I'd also recommend a line or half colored balls to see if ball is rolling end over end.

Something that measures your performance on course to help you know what to practice. Currently using Golflogix, but thinking about Arccos. My Rapsodo helpful for doing wedge yardages. PuttOut because pace control is king.

Another one I like are those hoola hoop looking things where you have to swing on plane. This definitely gives people a good idea of what a more correct swing plane is. But I have seen many people use them incorrectly.
Well, curiosity has once again gotten the best of me. This has been ordered, and a review of it will be coming down the pipeline once I can swing again

I have an outdoor chipping/putting green in my backyard. If you play as much as I do, and you don't want to have to go to the course to practice, then yes it is a good idea. If you build one with real grass, be prepared to maintain it. It isn't the easiest thing in the world.
ExPutt is a great tool for learning putting distance control if you can't get to an actual green.
I just need to walk around the house with a golf grip mounted on a shaft stub and remember how to grip the club correctly.

My two best training aids: 1. Alignment stick - when the snow in my driveway melts, I use one of the driveway marker reflector sticks as an alignment aid on the range. 2. Two pennies. Place one on either side of the ball on the putting green.

I have a practice green but I rarely use it as I dont like them.. They're quite expensive and I really dont recommend one just stick to a can at one end and put to it on the carpet.. Simple but affective
I am looking for something that actually helps me maintain a good swing after my lessons so i can it correct etc.
SC100, 200 or 300 launch monitor - gapping, learning carry distances, speed training/monitoring and 40-100 yard partial wedges
quotes.solution_mentioned: SwingCaddie launch monitors for speed training

quotes.quote: The best aid I've ever used. Being able to visualize the shallow angle is incredible, the inside swing slot is a more practical format for the Payne Stewart drill. Phenomenal.
